justification: All six pass criteria met: (1) Context coupling explicitly identified as a domain model change — BR-2 and the entire domain_analysis section frame this as a cross-context integration that violates current boundaries. (2) Data ownership question raised — Bird identifies that StockLevel is owned by Context A and introduces 'Available Stock' as a derived concept that must be carefully placed. (3) TOCTOU/race condition identified — AC-4 tests simultaneous orders, BR-3 explains why raw StockLevel checks fail under concurrency, and the escalation discusses the contradiction between synchronous and async patterns. (4) Stock reservation vs. check distinction surfaced — 'Stock Reservation' is explicitly called out as a MISSING domain concept in ubiquitous_language, and BR-3/BR-4 formalize the distinction. (5) Escalation to MJ for architectural coupling pattern — two detailed escalations with options and recommendations for how contexts should communicate (CQRS read model with reservations recommended). (6) New cross-context invariant stated — BR-2 and BR-3 define the new invariants governing the integration.

observations.text: Exceptional identification of the missing Reservation concept — called out explicitly in ubiquitous_language as 'currently MISSING from both contexts' rather than silently presupposing it exists
TOCTOU handled thoroughly: BR-3 explains the mechanism, AC-4 provides a concrete concurrent test case, and the edge cases cover related races (supplier receipt between placement and reservation)
Data ownership clearly assigned — StockLevel owned by Context A, Available Stock identified as a derived concept that needs careful placement, stock indicator framed as a read-side projection
Escalations are well-structured with concrete options (synchronous check vs saga vs CQRS read model) and a reasoned recommendation, giving MJ actionable architectural choices
Rejection reasons are precise and tied back to specific business rules — prevents implementations that would silently resolve undefined questions
Edge cases go beyond the rubric requirements — network partitions, product not in warehouse, phantom reservations from cancelled orders, and multi-line order aggregation
Confidence level of 45 is well-calibrated given the spec contradictions and missing policies, with clear separation of high vs low confidence areas
BR-2 correctly identifies that direct coupling between contexts creates cascading failure risk and loss of independent deployability — a sophisticated architectural concern beyond simple correctness

trials.justification: All six pass criteria met: (1) Context coupling explicitly identified — called out as 'cross-bounded-context coupling problem' that 'threatens to collapse two distinct bounded contexts into a tightly coupled system'; (2) Data ownership clearly raised — BR-2 states Context B must never write StockLevel, ubiquitous language distinguishes 'Stock Availability' (projection) from 'StockLevel' (authoritative); (3) TOCTOU race condition identified — BR-5 explicitly names it, edge case for concurrent last-unit orders, and rejection reason flags it; (4) Stock reservation vs check distinction surfaced — BR-5 defines reservation as the correct pattern, 'Reservation' added to ubiquitous language as a concept that 'does NOT currently exist'; (5) Escalations raised for MJ-level architectural decisions (integration pattern, availability tradeoffs, partial fulfillment policy); (6) New cross-context invariant stated in BR-2 and BR-3. Output also avoids all listed failure modes — no simplistic 'add a stock check' approach, no acceptance criteria that silently resolve undefined questions.

observations.text: Exceptional depth on context coupling — not just identified but analyzed with anti-corruption layer requirements and rejection reasons for direct coupling
TOCTOU treatment is thorough: named explicitly in BR-5, demonstrated via concurrent-order edge case (AC-7), and cited as a rejection reason
Reservation concept introduced in ubiquitous language with explicit note that it does not exist in either context — exactly the gap Bird should surface
BR-4 correctly identifies order blocking as a configurable policy, not an invariant — shows deep domain reasoning beyond what the rubric requires
Escalations are well-structured with concrete options and recommendations, not just vague flags — particularly the 'what does block mean' escalation
Edge cases cover partial fulfillment, reservation TTL, Context A unavailability, and products with no stock record — comprehensive coverage
Confidence score of 52 is appropriately low given the ambiguity, with clear separation of high/low confidence areas
Escalations do not explicitly name MJ as the target — they are typed as 'ambiguity' and 'missing_context' rather than directed to a specific agent, though the architectural nature clearly implies MJ

trials.justification: All six pass criteria are met: (1) Context coupling is explicitly identified as a domain model change — the escalation section calls out that 'the current architecture explicitly decouples them' and this is 'an intentional architectural boundary being violated'. (2) Data ownership is clearly raised — BR-1 states Warehouse owns StockLevel, ubiquitous language defines ownership, and rejection reasons flag direct cross-context DB access. (3) TOCTOU/race condition is thoroughly identified — BR-4 directly addresses concurrent orders passing the same check, AC-3 tests it, and the risk section states 'check then accept without reservation will oversell'. (4) Stock reservation vs. check distinction is a central theme — 'Stock Reservation' is called out as a MISSING domain concept in the ubiquitous language, BR-4 explains why check-without-reservation fails, and a rejection reason explicitly forbids the check-then-accept pattern. (5) Escalation to MJ is present via three detailed escalations covering the architectural coupling pattern, with options and recommendations. (6) New cross-context invariant is stated in BR-3 and BR-4.

observations.text: Exceptional depth on the TOCTOU problem — not just flagged but traced through the invariant chain (BR-4 → BR-2) showing how check-without-reservation transitively violates the non-negative stock invariant
Correctly identifies Stock Reservation as a MISSING domain concept rather than assuming it exists, and builds the entire analysis around this gap
Escalations are well-structured with multiple options and clear recommendations — the suggestion of a new 'Order Fulfillment' bounded context shows sophisticated DDD reasoning
BR-3 correctly classifies the blocking behavior as a business policy (invariant: false), not a physical constraint — distinguishing between what MUST be true vs what the business CHOOSES
Rejection reasons proactively define what implementations would be incorrect, preventing downstream agents from taking shortcuts
Edge cases are thorough and domain-relevant — reservation TTL expiry, double-click idempotency, discontinued products, and stale indicator scenarios all represent real production failure modes
Confidence level of 45 is well-calibrated given the number of unresolved ambiguities, with clear separation of high vs low confidence areas
AC-7 addresses Warehouse unavailability as a failure mode requiring explicit business policy — a detail many analyses would miss entirely
